title: The Central Bank Balance-Sheet Trilemma
summary: This FEDS Note presents a framework for understanding the tradeoffs central banks face in managing their balance sheets, introducing the "balance sheet trilemma" concept. The trilemma posits that central banks can achieve only two of three goals simultaneously: a small balance sheet, low volatility of short-term rates, and limited market intervention. The analysis draws on Fed balance sheet history from 2005-2025 and discusses implications for reserve management and money market dynamics.
key_points: Fed balance sheet grew from ~$800 billion (6% of GDP) in December 2005 to ~$6.5 trillion (21% of GDP) in December 2025
Balance sheet reduction concluded December 1, 2025; reserve management purchases announced December 10, 2025
The balance-sheet trilemma: central banks can achieve only two of three goals - small balance sheet, low rate volatility, and limited market intervention
As reserves fall relative to outstanding Treasuries, money market rate sensitivity to liquidity shocks increases significantly
The repo market spread (TGCR over ON RRP) becomes more responsive to TGA movements as reserves decline
Thin reserve buffers force banks to demand significant compensation, meaning even small shocks can cause outsized rate increases
why_it_matters: This research provides a rigorous analytical framework for understanding the fundamental tradeoffs in Fed balance sheet management. The trilemma framework helps market participants understand why the Fed cannot simultaneously minimize its balance sheet footprint and maintain stable money market rates without frequent intervention. This has direct implications for how the Fed will manage reserves going forward and the likely future trajectory of money market volatility.
market_structure_context: The note directly addresses market structure through its analysis of money market dynamics, reserve demand, repo market spreads (TGCR vs ON RRP), and the relationship between balance sheet size and rate volatility. It discusses operational frameworks, standing facilities (SRP, ON RRP, Discount Window), and how TGA movements affect reserve levels.
notes: Source is a high-quality Federal Reserve research note from January 2026, only 3 months old. Content is well-grounded with empirical data and citations to academic literature (Gissler et al. 2025, Cordes et al. 2025, Bostrom et al. 2025, etc.). The trilemma framework is analytically sound and directly relevant to current Fed operating framework debates. Published during TODAY window; considered recent.
